ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general french fries entry (FOODON_00002694); no specific parmesan garlic fries variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fried potato products broadly; no parmesan-topped variant code exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for parmesan garlic fries; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side serving of parmesan garlic fries is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a medium-sized side portion as served in casual dining restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Parmesan Garlic Fries — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Parmesan Garlic Fries (cr_id G00010127). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (USDA SR Legacy, french fries with cheese topping) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for parmesan garlic fries. GI of 75 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for french fries / fried potato (GI range 63–85, representative value 75 for deep-fried crinkle-cut potato). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (75) × available carbohydrate per serving (44.5 g/100g × 150 g serving = 66.75 g available carb) / 100 = 50.1 × 0.5 portion factor = 25.1 GL. Parmesan and aioli toppings do not materially alter the GI of the potato base.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 539 mg/100g with low potassium offset (132 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 539 mg/100g is near the 600 mg/100g high-concern threshold; the potassium-to-sodium ratio is unfavourable at approximately 0.24:1, well below the recommended ≥1:1 ratio, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension unless portion size is strictly limited to 50–75 g and no additional salt or high-sodium condiments are consumed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~75) and high carbohydrate load (44.5 g/100g) from deep-fried potato starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 75 combined with 44.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g produces a high glycemic load per serving (~25 GL); deep-frying further impairs insulin sensitivity via elevated fat content, making this dish inappropriate for regular consumption by individuals with type 2 diabetes — if consumed, portion should not exceed 75 g and must be paired with low-GI foods and adequate protein.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation with saturated fat (~2.8 g/100g), trans fat (0.2 g/100g), and high sodium (539 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat, trans fat from frying oil, and high sodium elevates LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk simultaneously; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish or limit intake to an occasional very small portion (≤75 g) while ensuring the rest of the meal is low in saturated fat and sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); trans fat 0.2 g/100g; potato is a nightshade vegetable; sodium 539 mg/100g exceeds 400 mg/100g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried preparation is a hard gate for rheumatoid arthritis unsuitability due to AGE formation that worsens joint inflammation; the presence of trans fat and nightshade potato further exacerbates pro-inflammatory pathways, making this dish contraindicated for individuals with active rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Parmesan Garlic Fries are a Western-style deep-fried potato side dish consisting of crinkle-cut potato fries dusted with grated parmesan cheese and served alongside a roasted garlic parmesan aioli dipping sauce. The crinkle-cut shape increases surface area, producing a crispy exterior with a soft, starchy interior. The parmesan cheese adds a salty, umami-rich coating, while the garlic aioli contributes creaminess and pungent garlic flavour. Per 100 g, this dish provides 343 kcal, 12.2 g protein, 12.9 g fat, and 44.5 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 539 mg. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 75 (high), making this dish a high-glycemic-load food unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or insulin resistance. The high sodium content raises concern for individuals with hypertension, and the deep-frying process introduces saturated fat and trans fat, elevating cardiovascular disease risk. This dish is popular as a side order in casual dining restaurants and fast-food outlets in Singapore and across Western markets. It is not suitable for vegan, halal-certified, or gluten-free diets due to the presence of dairy (parmesan, aioli), eggs (aioli), and potential gluten cross-contamination from shared frying equipment.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010127) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy for a comparable fried potato with cheese product. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for deep-fried potato. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for french fries with cheese topping (USDA NDB No. 21138 / closest analogue), as the HPB source does not report saturated fat breakdown. This is documented as an adapted value. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this specific HPB dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. trans_fat_g is set to 0.2 g/100g based on the input record's health_suitability section which references trans fat from frying oil; this is consistent with USDA data for deep-fried potato products and is treated as an estimated value. 4. water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish and are not available from a directly applicable approved source as of 2026-04-16. 5. Food subgroup corrected from 'Baking ingredients' (input error) to 'Fried potato dishes' to accurately reflect the dish category. 6. This is a Western-cuisine dish served in Singapore; multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable as this dish does not have established local-language names in Singapore — the English name is universally used across all language communities in the Singapore context. 7. Halal status is marked false as the aioli contains eggs and the frying oil source is unspecified; halal certification cannot be assumed without verified supplier documentation.
